naturalMeaning: The single most common Russian euphemism — a near-homophone substitute that lets a speaker vent frustration with the same rhythm and intonation as a much stronger word, without actually saying it. Safe in front of children, coworkers, and strangers.

examples.translation: Dang it, I forgot my keys again!

naturalMeaning: An emphatic, slightly theatrical extension of блин, used for a bigger dose of frustration without escalating to real mat.

translation: good grief / for crying out loud (lit. 'fir trees and sticks')
naturalMeaning: A cheerful-sounding, old-fashioned exasperation phrase — grandparents use it as readily as anyone, and it carries essentially zero risk in any company.

examples.translation: Good grief, the bus didn't come again.

naturalMeaning: A close variant of ёлки-палки with the same fully safe, slightly folksy exasperation tone.

translation: oh for goodness' sake (lit. 'yopern theater', nonsense phrase)
literalMeaning: a nonsense compound built to echo the sound of a much stronger curse
naturalMeaning: Built specifically so the first word echoes the sound-shape of a severe curse without being one — a purpose-built sound-alike euphemism, one of many in this family.
The classic, genuinely mild 'damn it' of Russian — devil-based cursing (as in English) reads as far less severe than mat, and this phrase is safe in almost any setting short of the most formal.

translation: damn it / confound it (lit. 'devil carry [it] off')
naturalMeaning: A near-synonym of чёрт возьми with a slightly more old-fashioned, literary flavor — both are entirely safe register.

translation: what on earth / what a mess (lit. 'devil-knows what')
naturalMeaning: Used about a confusing or badly botched situation rather than as a direct exclamation — 'this is a mess and I can't even describe it'.

One of the strangest-sounding entries in this family — the literal meaning has nothing to do with the feeling expressed. Popular theory ties it to a 19th-century diplomatic incident, but like many folk etymologies this is unverified; what matters for a learner is that it's a purely comic, fully safe exclamation today.
A euphemistic stand-in for a much coarser 'твою мать'-shaped curse, swapping 'mother' for an absurd military unit — the joke is entirely in the substitution.
Another purpose-built sound-alike, mild and slightly playful — safe around any audience, including children.

translation: good grief (lit. nonsense phrase referencing a folkloric bogeyman)
literalMeaning: invokes бабай, a bogeyman figure from Russian/Tatar folklore used to scare children
naturalMeaning: Folksy and dated-sounding, this one leans on the same 'echo the sound of a curse without being one' trick as ёперный театр.

translation: that's it / disaster (softened form of a severe exclamation)
literalMeaning: a softened, shortened re-coinage of a much more severe exclamation with the same rhythm
naturalMeaning: Extremely common in everyday speech to mean 'that's a disaster' or 'wow, that's intense' — carries real emphasis but is broadly considered acceptable even in mixed company, unlike the word it stands in for.

examples.text: Капец, я не ожидал такого счёта.
examples.romaji: Kapéts, ya nye azhidál takóva shchyóta.
examples.translation: Wow, I didn't expect a bill like that.

translation: that's it / disaster (softened form, same idea as капец)
literalMeaning: reshaped to resemble писать/письмо ('to write'/'letter') rather than the severe word it stands in for
naturalMeaning: A near-synonym of капец built by a similar one-letter substitution trick — still carries genuine emphasis, still meaningfully softer than the real word.
A rhyming euphemism that softens a coarser 'ядрёна'-based curse into a harmless comment about bread — the humor is the mismatch.
The sibling phrase to едрён батон, built the same way — swapping the taboo word for an absurd, harmless image while keeping the same sound and emphasis.

A modern, internet-and-youth-coded exclamation for something shocking, harsh, or intense — not a substitute for any single mat word, but fills the same 'strong reaction' conversational slot without any taboo content at all.

examples.translation: Whoa, did you see that crash?

naturalMeaning: An intensifier idiom rather than an exclamation — 'напугался до чёртиков' ('scared out of his wits'). Devil-based imagery again signals a mild, everyday-safe intensity rather than anything taboo.

examples.translation: I'm tired out of my mind.
